steps.title: Program Structure
hints: Every HLASM program needs a CSECT to mark the start and an END at the bottom. The END operand should match your CSECT label.
Columns matter: the name (label) goes in columns 1–8, the operation in columns 10–14, and operands from column 16. Use spaces to align.

steps.title: Data Definitions: DC and DS
hints: DC defines a constant and initialises the storage. DS only reserves space — the contents are undefined until you write to them.
Type codes: C = character, X = hexadecimal, F = fullword (4 bytes), H = halfword (2 bytes), B = binary. Length modifiers follow the type: DS CL80 reserves 80 bytes of character storage.

steps.title: Registers and Base Addressing
hints: BALR R12,0 loads the address of the next instruction into R12. USING *,R12 then tells the assembler to use R12 as the base register for all address calculations.
Register equates (R0 EQU 0, R1 EQU 1, ...) are just symbolic names — they make the code readable. Define them near the top of your CSECT.

steps.title: Loading and Storing Data
hints: L loads a 32-bit fullword from memory into a register. LR copies one register to another. LA loads an address (or small immediate value) — it never touches memory.
ST stores a register's 32-bit value to a memory fullword. Always ensure the target DS/DC is aligned on a 4-byte boundary for fullword operations.

steps.title: Moving Character Data
hints: MVC copies bytes from source to target — the length comes from the first operand's defined length. MVI moves a single immediate byte into a memory location.
MVC moves left-to-right, one byte at a time. This means MVC TARGET,TARGET+1 is a valid way to propagate a single character across a field (e.g., after an MVI).

steps.title: Integer Arithmetic
hints: A adds a fullword from memory to a register. AR adds two registers. S and SR are the subtract equivalents. Always load your values into registers first.
M (Multiply) uses a register pair — the multiplicand is in the odd register (e.g. R1), and the 64-bit product is placed in the even-odd pair (e.g. R0-R1). D (Divide) works the same way in reverse.

steps.title: Comparisons and Branching
hints: C compares a register to a fullword in memory; CR compares two registers. The comparison sets the condition code: 0 = equal, 1 = low, 2 = high.
BE branches on equal (CC=0), BL on low (CC=1), BH on high (CC=2), BNE on not-equal (CC≠0). B is unconditional. All branch targets must be labels defined in your CSECT.

steps.title: Standard OS Linkage Convention
hints: On entry, R13 points to the caller's 18-word (72-byte) save area, R14 is the return address, and R15 is your entry point. STM R14,R12,12(R13) saves all registers into the caller's save area.

Chain save areas by storing your save area address at offset +4 of the caller's save area, and storing the caller's R13 at offset +4 of yours. On exit, restore R13, then LM R14,R12,12(R13), then BR R14.
